narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your application. The admissions model we use predicts whether applicants like you are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, based on factors such as your early and later school grades, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether you'd study full-time. For you specifically, the model estimates an 86.7% chance of failing the bar exam on the first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—schools aim to admit students with a strong predicted likelihood of success to maintain high pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range for most students. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lowest group well below average, was the next biggest contributor toward the fail prediction. These early and later academic rankings stood out as the strongest signals to the model that bar passage might be challenging for you. Your LSAT score of 28, noticeably lower than the average of around 37, further nudged the prediction toward failure, followed by your middle family income bracket and undergraduate GPA of 2.50, both below typical levels. Even your plan to study full-time, which is common for most applicants, had a small push in the same direction, though it didn't carry much weight. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows lower standings across these key academic measures, making the model view your bar success odds as riskier. While this decision is tough, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, improving your GPA through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or prep courses tailored for bar success. Many students in similar situations have strengthened their profiles and succeeded later, and resources like targeted tutoring or community support for Black women in law can make a real difference. We're rooting for you to turn this around and reapply stronger.
